era_context: The final years before World War I, a period sometimes called the 'Long Edwardian Summer' in the West but marked by accelerating imperial rivalry, revolutionary ferment, and the disintegration of old orders. European empires dominated global affairs but faced rising nationalist movements in Asia, Africa, and the Middle East. Japan had emerged as a great power after its 1905 victory over Russia. China was in the final convulsions of the Qing dynasty. In Latin America, Mexico was on the brink of revolution. Industrialisation, mass migration, suffrage movements, and new technologies — aviation, cinema, wireless telegraphy — were transforming daily life worldwide.
geographic_coverage_gaps: Interior Sub-Saharan Africa (beyond colonial administrative records)
Central Asia under Russian imperial administration
Pacific Islands (limited documentation beyond colonial records)
Indigenous perspectives globally, filtered almost entirely through colonial sources

events.title: Union of South Africa established
events.description: On 31 May 1910, the Union of South Africa came into being as a self-governing dominion of the British Empire, merging the Cape Colony, Natal, the Transvaal, and the Orange River Colony under a single parliamentary government. Louis Botha, a former Boer War general, became the first prime minister. The constitution effectively disenfranchised the vast majority of the black, coloured, and Indian populations, entrenching white minority rule that would harden into apartheid by mid-century. The compromise between British and Afrikaner interests came at the direct expense of the non-white majority, a foundational injustice that shaped South African politics for the next 84 years.
key_figures: Louis Botha (first Prime Minister)
Jan Smuts (Minister of the Interior, Mines, and Defence)
John X. Merriman (last Prime Minister of the Cape Colony)
Mohandas K. Gandhi (leader of Indian community resistance in South Africa)

sources.name: South Africa Act 1909 (9 Edw. VII, c. 9), UK Parliament
Leonard Thompson, A History of South Africa (3rd ed., 2001)

events.certainty_note: Formally established by Act of Parliament with extensive official documentation.

events.title: Japan formally annexes Korea
events.description: On 22 August 1910, the Japan–Korea Annexation Treaty was signed under heavy Japanese military pressure, formally ending the 518-year Joseon dynasty and making Korea a colony of the Japanese Empire. Emperor Sunjong was forced to abdicate effective authority, and the Japanese Governor-General assumed total control. Korean resistance movements were suppressed, the Korean language and culture were systematically marginalised, and land was confiscated for Japanese settlers. The annexation lasted until Japan's surrender in 1945 and remains a deeply contentious issue in Korean–Japanese relations. Many Koreans consider the treaty illegitimate since it was signed under coercion.
key_figures: Emperor Sunjong (last Korean monarch)
Terauchi Masatake (first Japanese Governor-General of Korea)
Yi Wan-yong (Korean Prime Minister who signed the treaty under duress)
An Jung-geun (Korean independence activist who assassinated Itō Hirobumi in 1909)

sources.name: Japan–Korea Annexation Treaty text, 22 August 1910
Peter Duus, The Abacus and the Sword: The Japanese Penetration of Korea, 1895–1910 (1995)
Japanese Government-General of Korea, official gazette records, 1910

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in both Japanese and Korean sources. The treaty's legitimacy is contested, but its historical occurrence is not.

events.title: Mexican Revolution begins
events.description: On 20 November 1910, Francisco I. Madero's call to arms against the 34-year dictatorship of Porfirio Díaz ignited the Mexican Revolution, one of the first major social revolutions of the 20th century. Díaz had won a rigged election in June and jailed Madero, who escaped to the United States and issued the Plan de San Luis Potosí, declaring the election void and calling for revolt. Armed uprisings broke out across northern Mexico, with Pascual Orozco and Pancho Villa leading rebel forces in Chihuahua. The revolution would ultimately cost over a million lives, restructure Mexican society, produce the 1917 constitution, and shape Latin American politics for decades.
key_figures: Francisco I. Madero (revolutionary leader)
Porfirio Díaz (dictator since 1876)
Pancho Villa (revolutionary commander in Chihuahua)
Pascual Orozco (revolutionary military leader)
Emiliano Zapata (agrarian revolutionary in Morelos)

sources.name: Plan de San Luis Potosí, issued by Francisco Madero, 5 October 1910
John Womack Jr., Zapata and the Mexican Revolution (1968)
Friedrich Katz, The Life and Times of Pancho Villa (1998)

events.certainty_note: Exhaustively documented in Mexican, American, and European sources. The precise start date of 20 November is conventionally tied to Madero's Plan.

events.title: Death of Edward VII; George V becomes King
events.description: King Edward VII died on 6 May 1910, ending a nine-year reign that had seen significant shifts in British foreign policy, including the Entente Cordiale with France. His son George V ascended the throne, inheriting a constitutional crisis over the power of the House of Lords, an increasingly militant suffragette movement, the Irish Home Rule question, and naval rivalry with Germany. Edward's funeral on 20 May brought together an unprecedented gathering of European royalty — nine reigning kings rode in the procession — in what historian Barbara Tuchman later called 'the greatest assemblage of royalty and rank ever gathered in one place, and of its kind, the last.'
key_figures: Edward VII (King of the United Kingdom, d. 6 May 1910)
George V (successor, King from 6 May 1910)
H.H. Asquith (Prime Minister)

sources.name: The Times (London), 7–21 May 1910, coverage of death and funeral
Barbara Tuchman, The Guns of August (1962), ch. 1

events.certainty_note: Documented in official court records, parliamentary proceedings, and worldwide press coverage.

events.title: Portugal becomes a republic after revolution
events.description: On 5 October 1910, a military coup and popular uprising in Lisbon overthrew the Portuguese monarchy, establishing the Portuguese First Republic. King Manuel II fled to Gibraltar and then to England. The revolution was led by Republican Party activists and sympathetic military units, and followed a period of political instability, colonial crises, and the assassination of King Carlos I in 1908. The new republic disestablished the Catholic Church, expelled religious orders, and introduced secular reforms, but would prove politically unstable, experiencing over 40 government changes before its own overthrow in 1926.
key_figures: Manuel II (last King of Portugal, deposed)
Teófilo Braga (first president of the provisional government)
Afonso Costa (Republican leader and future prime minister)
Machado Santos (military leader of the revolt)

sources.name: Diário do Governo, October 1910 (official gazette announcing the republic)
Douglas Wheeler, Republican Portugal: A Political History, 1910–1926 (1978)

events.certainty_note: Thoroughly documented in Portuguese and international sources.

events.title: Halley's Comet perihelion and mass public reaction
events.description: Halley's Comet reached perihelion on 20 April 1910 and was widely visible through May and June, producing one of the most spectacular cometary displays of the modern era. Earth passed through the comet's tail on 18–19 May, triggering worldwide panic after spectroscopic analysis by astronomer Camille Flammarion suggested the tail contained cyanogen gas. Entrepreneurs sold 'comet pills' and gas masks; some communities sealed windows and doors. The event was simultaneously a triumph of predictive astronomy — Edmund Halley had predicted the comet's return in the 18th century — and an illustration of how scientific information could be distorted into mass hysteria.
key_figures: Camille Flammarion (French astronomer whose cyanogen remarks fuelled panic)
Edmund Halley (predicted the comet's periodicity, posthumously vindicated)
Edward Emerson Barnard (astronomer who photographed the comet at Yerkes Observatory)

sources.name: Publications of the Astronomical Society of the Pacific, Vol. 22 (1910), observations and spectroscopic reports
Contemporary newspaper coverage: The New York Times, Le Figaro, The Times (London), May 1910

events.certainty_note: Astronomical event precisely dated; social panic extensively documented in global press.

events.title: NAACP formally incorporated
events.description: The National Association for the Advancement of Colored People (NAACP) was formally incorporated on 19 May 1910 in New York, building on the National Negro Committee organised in 1909 in response to the 1908 Springfield race riot. Founded by a coalition including W.E.B. Du Bois, Ida B. Wells, Mary White Ovington, and Oswald Garrison Villard, the organisation adopted a strategy of legal challenges to racial discrimination and the publication of The Crisis magazine, edited by Du Bois. The NAACP would become the most influential civil rights organisation in American history, leading the legal strategy that culminated in Brown v. Board of Education (1954) and the Civil Rights Act of 1964.
key_figures: W.E.B. Du Bois (founding member, editor of The Crisis)
Ida B. Wells (founding member, anti-lynching activist)
Mary White Ovington (founding member)
Moorfield Storey (first NAACP president)

sources.name: NAACP incorporation papers, State of New York, 1910
Patricia Sullivan, Lift Every Voice: The NAACP and the Making of the Civil Rights Movement (2009)

events.certainty_note: Organisational records, founding documents, and extensive contemporary press coverage.

events.title: Great Flood of Paris
events.description: In late January 1910, the Seine river reached its highest level since 1658, cresting at 8.62 metres on the Pont d'Austerlitz gauge on 28 January. The flood inundated much of central Paris for weeks, disabling the Métro system, contaminating water supplies, and displacing tens of thousands of residents. The Musée du Louvre and the Gare d'Orsay were partially submerged. Army engineers and thousands of volunteers conducted rescue operations by boat through city streets. The flood exposed the vulnerability of Paris's infrastructure and prompted major civil engineering reforms, including the construction of upstream reservoirs that were completed in the mid-20th century.
key_figures: Armand Fallières (President of France)
Louis Lépine (Prefect of Police, managed emergency response)

sources.name: Le Petit Journal, Le Figaro, and L'Illustration, January–February 1910, extensive photographic and written coverage
Jeffrey Jackson, Paris Under Water: How the City of Light Survived the Great Flood of 1910 (2010)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented with photographs, hydrological measurements, and municipal records.

events.title: Montenegro becomes a kingdom
events.description: On 28 August 1910, Prince Nicholas I proclaimed Montenegro a kingdom, elevating his principality's status in the hierarchy of European states. The move was partly a response to Austria-Hungary's 1908 annexation of Bosnia-Herzegovina, which had heightened Balkan tensions. Montenegro had been an independent principality since the Congress of Berlin in 1878. Nicholas I simultaneously promulgated Montenegro's first constitution, though his rule remained effectively autocratic. The kingdom would last only eight years before being absorbed into the Kingdom of Serbs, Croats and Slovenes in 1918.
key_figures: Nicholas I (King of Montenegro)

sources.name: Montenegrin royal proclamation, 28 August 1910; contemporary diplomatic correspondence
Elizabeth Roberts, Realm of the Black Mountain: A History of Montenegro (2007)

events.certainty_note: Documented in official Montenegrin records and international diplomatic correspondence.

events.title: First flight from a ship's deck
events.description: On 14 November 1910, civilian pilot Eugene Ely flew a Curtiss pusher biplane off a temporary wooden platform built on the bow of the cruiser USS Birmingham in Hampton Roads, Virginia. The aircraft dipped towards the water after leaving the 83-foot platform but recovered and landed safely on shore. This was the first time an aeroplane had taken off from a ship, demonstrating the feasibility of naval aviation. Ely followed this in January 1911 by landing on the USS Pennsylvania. These experiments laid the conceptual groundwork for aircraft carriers, which would transform naval warfare within three decades.
key_figures: Eugene Ely (civilian pilot)
Captain Washington Irving Chambers (US Navy aviation advocate who organised the experiment)

sources.name: US Navy Bureau of Navigation records and official reports, November 1910
Richard P. Hallion, Taking Flight: Inventing the Aerial Age from Antiquity Through the First World War (2003)

events.certainty_note: Documented by Navy records, photographs, and contemporary press accounts.

events.title: Japan suppresses the Great Treason Incident (Taigyaku Jiken)
events.description: In 1910, Japanese authorities arrested hundreds of socialists and anarchists in connection with an alleged plot to assassinate Emperor Meiji. The so-called Great Treason Incident (Taigyaku Jiken) centred on anarchist Kōtoku Shūsui and a small group who had discussed manufacturing bombs. Twenty-six people were tried in secret by the Court of Cassation; on 18 January 1911, twelve were executed, including Kōtoku and his partner Kanno Sugako. The incident was almost certainly exaggerated by the government to justify a broad crackdown on left-wing movements. It effectively destroyed the nascent Japanese socialist movement for over a decade and established a precedent for political repression.
key_figures: Kōtoku Shūsui (anarchist writer and activist, executed)
Kanno Sugako (anarchist and feminist, executed)
Hiranuma Kiichirō (prosecutor who led the case)

sources.name: Court of Cassation trial records, 1910–1911 (partially declassified)
F.G. Notehelfer, Kōtoku Shūsui: Portrait of a Japanese Radical (1971)

events.certainty_note: Trial and executions are documented. The extent of the actual conspiracy remains disputed — most historians believe it was grossly inflated by the state.

events.title: Albanian revolt against Ottoman rule
events.description: In spring 1910, Albanian highlanders in Kosovo and northern Albania revolted against the Young Turk government's centralisation policies, including new taxation, conscription, and a ban on carrying weapons. Ottoman forces under Shevket Turgut Pasha conducted a large military campaign with approximately 16,000 troops to suppress the uprising, destroying villages and disarming the population. The revolt was a precursor to the larger 1912 Albanian uprising and the subsequent declaration of Albanian independence in November 1912. It reflected broader tensions between Ottoman imperial centralisation and the nationalist aspirations of subject peoples in the Balkans.
key_figures: Isa Boletini (Albanian rebel leader)
Shevket Turgut Pasha (Ottoman military commander)

sources.name: Ottoman military dispatches and administrative correspondence, 1910
Noel Malcolm, Kosovo: A Short History (1998)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Ottoman military records, European diplomatic correspondence, and Albanian oral histories.

events.title: Rubber terror in the Belgian Congo and Peruvian Amazon exposed
events.description: By 1910, international pressure over atrocities committed in rubber-producing regions reached a crescendo. The Belgian government had formally taken over the Congo Free State from Leopold II in 1908, but abuses continued. Simultaneously, Roger Casement, who had exposed Congo atrocities in 1904, was investigating comparable horrors in the Peruvian Amazon rubber industry run by the Peruvian Amazon Company (Julio César Arana's operation). Casement's Putumayo report, presented to the British Foreign Office in 1910–1911, documented systematic torture, mutilation, and murder of indigenous Huitoto, Bora, and Andoque people. These twin exposés represented early examples of international human rights activism.
key_figures: Roger Casement (British consul, investigator)
E.D. Morel (journalist and Congo Reform Association leader)
Julio César Arana (Peruvian rubber baron)

sources.name: Roger Casement, Putumayo report to the British Foreign Office, 1910–1911 (FO 371 series, The National Archives)
Adam Hochschild, King Leopold's Ghost (1998)

events.certainty_note: Casement's reports are preserved in British Foreign Office archives. The scale of atrocities is extensively corroborated.

events.title: Chinese Qing government abolishes slavery
events.description: In 1910, the Qing government issued edicts formally abolishing all forms of hereditary servitude and the slave trade within the Chinese empire, as part of the late Qing reform programme (Xinzheng). The reforms aimed to modernise China's legal system along Western lines and addressed multiple forms of bondage including hereditary household slavery (nupu), indentured labour, and debt bondage. Implementation was uneven across China's vast territory, and many forms of coerced labour persisted well beyond the formal abolition. The reforms came too late to save the dynasty, which fell in the 1911 revolution.
key_figures: Zaifeng, Prince Chun (regent for the child Xuantong Emperor)

sources.name: Qing government reform edicts, 1909–1910, as compiled in Da Qing lichao shilu
Melissa Macauley, 'Bondage and the Law in Late Imperial China', in Martin Klein (ed.), Breaking the Chains (1993)

events.certainty_note: Edicts are preserved in official Qing records. Practical implementation varied greatly.

events.title: Tonghak-inspired Korean independence movement suppressed
events.description: In the months before and after the formal annexation, Korean resistance movements — including remnants of the Righteous Army (uibyeong) guerrilla movement — intensified their resistance to Japanese encroachment. Japanese military and police forces conducted extensive 'pacification' campaigns throughout 1910, killing or imprisoning thousands of Korean resisters. Japanese authorities also dismantled Korean political organisations, shut down Korean-language newspapers, and imposed press censorship. These suppressions drove the Korean independence movement underground and into exile communities in Manchuria, Siberia, and the United States.
key_figures: An Chang-ho (Korean independence activist, exiled to US)
Yi Dong-hwi (independence leader, later active in Manchuria)

sources.name: Japanese Government-General of Korea, security reports 1910
Chong-Sik Lee, The Politics of Korean Nationalism (1963)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Japanese colonial records and Korean exile accounts, though Korean perspectives were systematically suppressed at the time.

events.title: British constitutional crisis over the People's Budget
events.description: Britain's constitutional crisis over the House of Lords' veto power intensified in 1910, leading to two general elections (January and December). The crisis had begun in 1909 when the Lords rejected David Lloyd George's radical 'People's Budget,' which proposed land taxes and increased levies on the wealthy to fund social welfare programmes and naval construction. The January 1910 election returned the Liberals with a reduced majority dependent on Irish Nationalist and Labour support. After Edward VII's death, a Constitutional Conference failed to resolve the impasse, leading to the December election that again returned the Liberals. The crisis would be resolved by the Parliament Act 1911, permanently curtailing the Lords' veto power.
key_figures: David Lloyd George (Chancellor of the Exchequer)
H.H. Asquith (Prime Minister)
Arthur Balfour (Conservative leader)

sources.name: Hansard, Parliamentary Debates, 1910
George Dangerfield, The Strange Death of Liberal England (1935)

events.certainty_note: Extensively documented in parliamentary records and contemporary press.

events.title: Principia Mathematica Volume I published
events.description: In December 1910, Alfred North Whitehead and Bertrand Russell published the first volume of Principia Mathematica, one of the most important works in the history of mathematics and logic. The work attempted to derive all mathematical truths from a set of axioms and inference rules using symbolic logic, building on but going far beyond Gottlob Frege's earlier work. Though the programme's ultimate goal was shown to be impossible by Kurt Gödel's incompleteness theorems in 1931, Principia Mathematica profoundly influenced 20th-century philosophy, mathematical logic, and the foundations of computer science. Volumes II and III followed in 1912 and 1913.
key_figures: Bertrand Russell (philosopher and mathematician)
Alfred North Whitehead (philosopher and mathematician)

sources.name: Alfred North Whitehead and Bertrand Russell, Principia Mathematica, Vol. I (Cambridge University Press, 1910)
Ivor Grattan-Guinness, The Search for Mathematical Roots, 1870–1940 (2000)

events.certainty_note: Publication date established by Cambridge University Press records.

events.title: International convention on white slavery (trafficking)
events.description: On 4 May 1910, thirteen nations signed the International Convention for the Suppression of the White Slave Traffic in Paris, building on a weaker 1904 agreement. The convention required signatory states to criminalise the procurement of women and girls for prostitution abroad, even with their consent, and established mechanisms for international cooperation in prosecution. While limited in scope and marred by racist terminology — 'white slavery' reflected the era's racial hierarchy and ignored trafficking of non-white women — the convention represented an early step toward international human trafficking law and the later UN frameworks.

sources.name: International Convention for the Suppression of the White Slave Traffic, 4 May 1910, League of Nations Treaty Series
Stephanie Limoncelli, The Politics of Trafficking: The First International Movement to Combat the Sexual Exploitation of Women (2010)

events.certainty_note: Treaty text preserved in international archives.

events.title: Italian Nationalist Association founded
events.description: In December 1910, the Associazione Nazionalista Italiana (ANI) was formally founded at a congress in Florence, consolidating various Italian nationalist currents into a single political movement. Led by Enrico Corradini and others, the ANI advocated aggressive colonial expansion, irredentism (claiming Italian-speaking territories under Austrian rule), and a vision of Italy as a 'proletarian nation' that deserved imperial status. The ANI would push Italy toward the 1911 invasion of Libya and intervention in World War I, and its ideology directly fed into Mussolini's Fascist movement — the ANI formally merged with the Fascist Party in 1923.
key_figures: Enrico Corradini (ANI ideologue and leader)
Luigi Federzoni (nationalist politician)

sources.name: Proceedings of the First Italian Nationalist Congress, Florence, December 1910
Alexander De Grand, The Italian Nationalist Association and the Rise of Fascism in Italy (1978)

events.certainty_note: Congress proceedings and contemporary press coverage survive.

events.title: First public radio broadcast (disputed)
events.description: On 13 January 1910, Lee De Forest broadcast a live performance by tenor Enrico Caruso and other Metropolitan Opera singers from the stage of the Metropolitan Opera House in New York to listeners on wireless receivers. This is sometimes cited as the first public radio broadcast, though the claim is contested — the 'audience' was small, reception was poor, and the demonstration was more promotional than practical. Regardless of priority disputes, the experiment demonstrated the potential of wireless voice transmission for entertainment and foreshadowed the radio broadcasting revolution of the 1920s.
key_figures: Lee De Forest (inventor and radio pioneer)
Enrico Caruso (tenor)

sources.name: The New York Times, 14 January 1910, report on the wireless broadcast
Susan Douglas, Inventing American Broadcasting, 1899–1922 (1987)

events.certainty_note: The broadcast occurred but its characterisation as the 'first public radio broadcast' is debated. Priority claims in early radio are notoriously contested.

events.title: Eighth Congress of the Second International in Copenhagen
events.description: The Eighth Congress of the Second International met in Copenhagen from 28 August to 3 September 1910, bringing together socialist and labour parties from across Europe and beyond. The congress debated strategies against militarism and war, affirmed the commitment to women's suffrage, and — at the proposal of Clara Zetkin — designated 8 March as International Women's Day. The congress also debated the growing tension between reformist and revolutionary wings of the socialist movement. The International's pledges of working-class solidarity against war would prove hollow when most socialist parties supported their national governments in August 1914.
key_figures: Clara Zetkin (German socialist, proposed International Women's Day)
Rosa Luxemburg (Polish-German revolutionary socialist)
Jean Jaurès (French Socialist Party leader)

sources.name: Official proceedings of the Eighth International Socialist Congress, Copenhagen, 1910
James Joll, The Second International, 1889–1914 (1955)

events.certainty_note: Congress proceedings published contemporaneously. Note: the precise connection between this congress and the later establishment of 8 March as International Women's Day involves some historical simplification — see disconfirming evidence.

events.title: Bambatha Rebellion aftermath and Poll Tax resistance in Natal
events.description: In the years following the suppression of the 1906 Bambatha Rebellion and in the context of the new Union of South Africa's formation, Zulu and other African communities in Natal continued passive resistance to the poll tax that had triggered the original uprising. The new Union government maintained the tax and strengthened pass laws restricting African movement and labour. The experience of the Bambatha Rebellion and its aftermath radicalised a generation of African political leaders and contributed to the founding of the South African Native National Congress (later the ANC) in 1912.
key_figures: John Dube (Zulu intellectual, future first ANC president)
Pixley ka Isaka Seme (lawyer, ANC co-founder)

sources.name: Natal colonial and Union of South Africa administrative records, 1910
Shula Marks, Reluctant Rebellion: The 1906–1908 Disturbances in Natal (1970)

events.certainty_note: Administrative records and later African political memoirs document ongoing resistance. Exact events are less clearly delineated than the 1906 rebellion itself.

events.title: Revolutionaries establish provisional government in Iran
events.description: In the aftermath of the Constitutional Revolution (1905–1911), Iran in 1910 was under the restored constitutional government but in deep crisis. The Majles (parliament) struggled to govern as British and Russian spheres of influence under the 1907 Anglo-Russian Convention carved the country into zones of control. The government hired American financial adviser Morgan Shuster to reorganise Iranian finances, a move that provoked Russian hostility. Meanwhile, tribal and regional power centres — Bakhtiari khans, Kurdish chiefs, and local warlords — operated with considerable autonomy. The constitutional experiment was slowly being strangled by great-power interference.
key_figures: Morgan Shuster (American financial adviser to Iran)
Muhammad Ali Shah Qajar (deposed Shah, attempted counter-revolution from Russia)

sources.name: Morgan Shuster, The Strangling of Persia (1912)
Janet Afary, The Iranian Constitutional Revolution, 1906–1911 (1996)

events.certainty_note: Documented in Iranian parliamentary records, British and Russian diplomatic archives, and Shuster's own memoir.

disconfirming_evidence: Several events listed above contain contested elements. (1) The Japan–Korea Annexation Treaty (1910-002): Korean and some international scholars argue the treaty is legally void ab initio because it was signed under military coercion and the Korean emperor's seal may have been affixed without his direct consent. Japan's position historically was that the treaty was valid under international law of the period. This remains diplomatically unresolved. (2) The Mexican Revolution's start date (1910-003): While 20 November 1910 is the conventional date, armed uprisings did not actually begin on that day in most locations. The Aquiles Serdán conspiracy in Puebla was discovered on 18 November, and effective military operations did not begin until early 1911. The 20 November date is partly a political construction. (3) The first radio broadcast claim (1910-020): De Forest's 1910 broadcast is one of several competing claims for the 'first' radio broadcast. Reginald Fessenden's 1906 transmission is often given priority, and Charles Herrold's regular broadcasts from San Jose may predate De Forest's. The concept of 'first broadcast' is itself anachronistic. (4) International Women's Day (1910-021): The link between the 1910 Copenhagen congress and 8 March specifically is historically muddled. The congress approved the concept of an international women's day but did not specify 8 March. The date became fixed only later, partly through Soviet practice after 1917, and the connection to a supposed 1857 New York garment workers' strike is a later fabrication. (5) Rubber atrocities (1910-013): Some Belgian historians have argued that the death toll in the Congo under Leopold II has been exaggerated by certain popular accounts (particularly the '10 million' figure), though no serious scholar disputes that mass atrocities occurred. The debate is over scale, not occurrence.
historiographic_note: 1910 is extremely well documented in European, American, and Japanese archives. The principal historiographic bias is the overwhelming dominance of European and American perspectives. Events in Africa, the Middle East, and Central Asia are primarily recorded through colonial administrative documents, which reflect the concerns and worldviews of the colonisers. Korean perspectives on the annexation were systematically suppressed under Japanese rule and had to be reconstructed from exile accounts and post-1945 scholarship. Mexican revolutionary history was heavily mythologised by successive post-revolutionary governments. The year is close enough to the present that many primary documents survive, but distant enough that living memory has vanished and the dominant narratives were shaped by the interests of imperial powers.

graph_edges.note: The Union's racially exclusionary constitution laid the legal and political foundations for the apartheid system formalised in 1948.
Japanese annexation provoked the March First Independence Movement of 1919, one of the largest anti-colonial protests in East Asian history.
The revolution that began in 1910 produced the radical 1917 Mexican Constitution with its labour, land reform, and anti-clerical provisions.
The First Republic's chronic instability led to the 1926 military coup and eventually the Salazar dictatorship.
The NAACP's legal strategy, begun at its founding, culminated in the landmark Brown v. Board of Education desegregation ruling.
Montenegro's elevation to a kingdom and Balkan tensions fed directly into the First Balkan War of 1912.
Ely's takeoff experiment was followed by his landing on the USS Pennsylvania in January 1911, completing the proof of concept for carrier aviation.
The 1910 constitutional crisis was resolved by the Parliament Act 1911, permanently subordinating the House of Lords to the Commons.
Gödel's incompleteness theorems demonstrated the impossibility of the foundational programme Principia Mathematica attempted.
The Italian Nationalist Association's pressure for colonial expansion contributed directly to Italy's invasion of Ottoman Libya in 1911.
The 1910 Albanian revolt was a precursor to the larger 1912 uprising and Albanian declaration of independence.
The Second International's anti-war resolutions at Copenhagen proved hollow when socialist parties backed their governments in 1914.
Russia's ultimatum forcing Shuster's dismissal in 1911 effectively ended Iran's constitutional experiment.
